summary: The primitive streak appears on Day 14 in the caudal midline of the embryonic disc.
The primitive streak appears on Day 7 in the cranial midline.
The primitive streak appears on Day 21 in the lateral plate mesoderm.
Gastrulation converts the bilaminar disc into a trilaminar disc with ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm.
Gastrulation converts the trilaminar disc into a bilaminar disc.
Gastrulation occurs during the first week of development before implantation.
The primitive node (Hensen's node) at the cranial end of the primitive streak is the organiser of the embryo.
The primitive node is located at the caudal end of the primitive streak.
The primitive node gives rise to the trophoblast and placental tissues.
Failure of the primitive streak to regress can lead to a sacrococcygeal teratoma.
Failure of primitive streak regression causes anencephaly.
The primitive streak persists throughout fetal life as the coccyx.
The notochord induces the overlying ectoderm to form the neural plate, the first step of neurulation.
The notochord induces the endoderm to form the neural plate.
The neural plate forms independently without any inductive signal.
Failure of neural tube closure at the cranial end results in anencephaly, while caudal failure causes spina bifida.
Failure of neural tube closure at the cranial end causes spina bifida.
Neural tube defects are prevented by vitamin B12 supplementation.
Somites are segmented blocks of paraxial mesoderm that give rise to vertebrae, skeletal muscles, and dermis.
Somites are derived from lateral plate mesoderm and form the body cavities.
Somites form the lining of the gut and respiratory tract.
